_meta.authoringNotes: Cross-checked against AAO BCSC Section 7 (Oculoplastics), 2024; Ophthalmic Plastic and Reconstructive Surgery journal reviews; Kelly's original 1958 punctoplasty description; and contemporary systematic reviews. Surgical success figures from Hii 2018 and Caesar systematic review.

h1Title: Punctal Eversion (Ectropion of Lacrimal Punctum): Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Punctal eversion is a malposition of the lower (occasionally upper) lacrimal punctum, the tiny drainage opening at the inner corner of each eyelid that normally sits flush against the eye and pulls tears into the lacrimal sac. When the punctum rotates outward and loses contact with the tear film, the eye cannot drain, and tears spill over the cheek in a symptom called epiphora. The condition affects an estimated 7-15% of adults over age 70, rises sharply with age-related lid laxity, and is frequently seen after Bell's palsy, after eyelid surgery, and in chronic eyelid skin disease. Treatment ranges from lubrication and warm compresses for mild cases to outpatient punctoplasty or medial spindle surgery for persistent tearing, with 80-95% symptom improvement reported across published series.

keyStats.prevalence: Estimated 7-15% of adults over age 70 with bothersome epiphora attributable to punctal or lid malposition (Mathers 1996; population eye exam surveys)
keyStats.demographics: Older adults predominate; men and women affected roughly equally, with a small female excess after age 75 due to longer life expectancy
keyStats.avgAge: Most symptomatic presentations after age 60; cicatricial and paralytic cases occur at any age
keyStats.globalCases: Tens of millions worldwide; specific epidemiology under-reported because mild epiphora often goes unaddressed

definition: Punctal eversion (ICD-10: H04.52), also called ectropion of the lacrimal punctum, is a structural malposition in which the lower eyelid punctum rotates away from the globe and loses its normal apposition with the tear meniscus (lacus lacrimalis). The punctum is a 0.3 mm opening at the medial end of each eyelid margin, the entry point of the lacrimal drainage system. In a healthy eye it sits submerged in the tear lake, where the orbicularis oculi muscle pumps tears into the canaliculus, lacrimal sac, and nasolacrimal duct during blinking. When the punctum everts, this physical pathway is broken even if the downstream lacrimal system remains anatomically patent. The result is hypersecretory epiphora — overflow tearing — accompanied by reflex tearing as the cornea attempts to compensate for an unstable tear film. Punctal eversion is graded clinically: mild (punctum visible only on lid eversion), moderate (visible without manipulation but partially in contact with globe), and severe (punctum frankly outward-facing, often with associated lower lid ectropion). The condition is managed by general ophthalmologists for early disease and by oculoplastic surgeons when surgical correction is required. It is distinct from primary lacrimal drainage obstruction, where the channel itself is blocked.

typesClassification.description: By far the most common form. Caused by horizontal lid laxity, attenuation of the medial and lateral canthal tendons, and weakening of the orbicularis oculi muscle that accompany aging. Often coexists with lower lid ectropion.

Scarring of the anterior lamella (skin and orbicularis) pulls the lid margin outward. Causes include post-traumatic scarring, over-resection during lower blepharoplasty, chronic atopic or contact dermatitis, herpes zoster ophthalmicus, and previous radiotherapy.

Follows facial nerve (CN VII) palsy, classically Bell's palsy. Loss of orbicularis oculi tone removes the medial traction that normally holds the punctum against the globe; the lower lid sags and the punctum points outward.

A space-occupying lesion of the lid margin — chalazion, eyelid tumor, large papilloma — physically displaces the punctum from the globe. Resolution typically follows treatment of the underlying lesion.
Rare. Seen in newborns with Down syndrome, congenital lower lid ectropion, or ichthyosiform skin disorders. Usually self-resolves or is corrected at the same time as the underlying lid abnormality.

primarySymptoms: Constant overflow tearing (epiphora) that runs down the cheek, often worse outdoors, in cold or windy weather, and when reading or using screens.
Mucoid or stringy discharge collecting at the inner corner of the eye, particularly noticeable on waking.
Maceration, redness, and breakdown of the skin at the inner canthus and along the lower lid from chronic tear contact.
Visible outward rotation of the punctum on the lower lid margin — the small pinhole faces forward or upward rather than inward toward the eye.
Blurred or fluctuating vision because the irregular tear film disrupts the smooth optical surface of the cornea.
Eye irritation, foreign-body sensation, and reflex tearing that paradoxically worsens the overflow as the cornea attempts to lubricate itself.
Repeated need to dab or wipe the eye through the day, often causing further mechanical trauma to the lid skin.
Cosmetic concern over a reddened lid margin and constantly wet eye, which patients commonly describe as socially embarrassing.
Recurrent conjunctivitis or chronic low-grade redness of the eye from disrupted tear clearance and bacterial overgrowth.
In paralytic cases, an obvious lower lid droop and inability to close the eye fully (lagophthalmos) on the affected side.
earlyWarningSigns: Tearing that begins gradually and is worse in wind or cold, especially in adults over 60
A noticeable pulling-down of the lower lid when looking in the mirror, with the inner pink edge becoming visible
Recurrent crusting at the inner corner of the eye without redness of the white of the eye
Persistent damp skin or eczema-like changes just below the inner corner of the lower eyelid
emergencySigns: Sudden severe eye pain, light sensitivity, and reduced vision — possible exposure keratitis or corneal ulcer that requires same-day ophthalmology assessment
New-onset facial droop with watery eye — possible Bell's palsy or stroke; the latter is a medical emergency
Rapidly enlarging mass on the eyelid distorting the punctum or lid margin — suspicious for eyelid tumor and warrants urgent specialist review
Painful, swollen, red inner canthus with fever — possible dacryocystitis (lacrimal sac infection) requiring prompt antibiotics

causes.description: Stretching of the lateral and medial canthal tendons and atrophy of the orbicularis oculi muscle allow the lower lid to fall away from the globe. This is the dominant cause in adults over 60 and underlies most involutional cases.

Bell's palsy, post-stroke facial weakness, post-surgical facial nerve injury (parotidectomy, acoustic neuroma resection) and Lyme-associated facial palsy all weaken orbicularis oculi tone, releasing the medial traction that holds the punctum in place.

Scarring of the lid skin and orbicularis pulls the lid margin outward. Causes include trauma, over-aggressive lower blepharoplasty (skin pinch or transcutaneous approach with excess skin removal), herpes zoster ophthalmicus, and severe long-standing eczema or contact dermatitis.

Atopic dermatitis, seborrheic dermatitis, allergic contact dermatitis to cosmetics or eye drops, and chronic blepharitis all cause skin thickening, scratching, and contracture that distorts the lid margin and pulls the punctum away from the globe.

causes.cause: Lid margin tumors or mass lesions
causes.description: Basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, sebaceous gland carcinoma, and large benign lesions such as chalazia or papillomas can physically displace the punctum from its normal apposition with the eye.

Excess skin resection during cosmetic lower blepharoplasty is a leading surgical cause. Other contributors include scarring from chemical peels, ablative laser resurfacing, radiotherapy for periocular skin cancer, and previous lid trauma repair.

Rare congenital ectropion seen in Down syndrome, blepharophimosis syndrome, ichthyosis, and other rare genetic skin disorders. Usually corrects with treatment of the underlying lid abnormality in childhood.

riskFactors.description: Loss of canthal tendon elasticity and orbicularis tone is progressive after age 50 and accelerates in the seventh and eighth decades.

riskFactors.factor: Previous lower lid surgery or trauma
riskFactors.description: Skin loss or scarring from cosmetic blepharoplasty, lid laceration repair, or skin cancer excision predisposes to anterior lamellar contraction and punctal eversion.

riskFactors.factor: History of Bell's palsy or other facial nerve disorder
riskFactors.description: Even after most facial nerve palsies recover, residual orbicularis weakness leaves the lower lid and punctum at risk; up to 30% of patients have persistent lid malposition at one year.

Atopic and contact dermatitis on the lids drives chronic inflammation, scratching, and skin contracture. Identifying and avoiding triggers (cosmetics, preservatives in eye drops) prevents progression.
Cumulative UV exposure accelerates dermal elastosis around the eyes and increases lid laxity. It also raises the risk of periocular skin cancers that mechanically distort the lid margin.
Shingles in the ophthalmic division of the trigeminal nerve produces dense lid scarring; cicatricial ectropion is reported in 5-10% of survivors.

riskFactors.factor: Long-term use of preserved topical eye drops
riskFactors.description: Benzalkonium chloride and other preservatives in glaucoma and dry-eye drops can cause chronic allergic contact dermatitis of the lids, contributing to skin contracture and punctal eversion.
Ehlers-Danlos syndrome, cutis laxa, and floppy eyelid syndrome (associated with obstructive sleep apnea) all increase baseline lid laxity and punctal malposition risk.
Tobacco smoke accelerates dermal elastin breakdown and is independently associated with earlier and more severe periorbital skin laxity.

riskFactors.factor: Significant weight loss in older adults
riskFactors.description: Rapid or substantial loss of facial fat in older patients exaggerates lid laxity and can unmask previously subclinical punctal eversion.

affectedDemographics: Adults over 70 carry the largest disease burden; prevalence of bothersome epiphora rises from under 2% at age 40 to over 20% by age 80
Patients with a history of facial nerve palsy, including post-Bell's palsy and post-stroke survivors, are at elevated lifetime risk
Cosmetic surgery cohorts: punctal eversion is reported in 1-5% of lower transcutaneous blepharoplasty cases as a complication
Fair-skinned populations with high cumulative sun exposure show higher rates of lid laxity and skin cancer-related cicatricial ectropion
Rare in childhood — congenital cases typically come to attention in infancy and are managed in pediatric ophthalmology
diagnosisOverview: Punctal eversion is diagnosed clinically at the slit lamp by an ophthalmologist or oculoplastic surgeon. The defining finding is loss of normal apposition between the lower punctum and the globe — the punctum is visible without manipulating the lid and faces outward or upward rather than into the tear lake. The examiner inspects both upper and lower puncta, grades severity, and looks for associated lower lid ectropion, lid retraction, and orbicularis weakness. The snap-back test assesses lid tone: the lower lid is pulled down and released; a healthy lid snaps back against the globe immediately, while a lax lid returns slowly or only after a blink. The distraction test measures horizontal laxity — distraction of the lid more than 6 mm from the globe indicates significant laxity. Schirmer testing quantifies tear production and is often paradoxically high in reflex hypersecretion. Fluorescein dye disappearance test confirms functional drainage failure: a drop of fluorescein placed in the conjunctival sac should clear within 5 minutes; persistence at 10 minutes is abnormal. Syringing through the punctum confirms anatomical patency of the lacrimal drainage system; the failure in punctal eversion is functional, not obstructive, so saline flows freely once it enters the punctum. Slit-lamp inspection of the cornea checks for exposure keratopathy. Imaging (CT dacryocystography, MRI) is reserved for atypical or post-traumatic cases where downstream obstruction is suspected.

diagnosticTests.test: Slit-lamp biomicroscopy of the lid margin and punctum
diagnosticTests.purpose: Direct visualization of punctal position, lid margin contour, and any associated ectropion, lid retraction, or lash abnormality. Establishes the diagnosis and severity grade.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Painless 5-minute examination at a slit lamp. The clinician asks the patient to look in different directions and may gently retract the lid to inspect the punctum. No drops are needed unless additional staining is planned.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantify horizontal lower lid laxity and orbicularis tone, which guides whether simple punctoplasty alone will suffice or whether lid-tightening procedures (lateral tarsal strip) are needed.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The examiner pulls the lower lid downward (snap-back) or away from the globe (distraction) and observes how quickly it returns. Each test takes seconds and may feel briefly uncomfortable but is not painful.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Functional test of tear drainage. Helps distinguish punctal eversion (drainage failure) from primary dry eye with reflex tearing.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A single drop of fluorescein is instilled. The clinician inspects under a cobalt-blue light at 5 and 10 minutes. Normal eyes clear most of the dye within 5 minutes; persistence indicates drainage failure.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Tests anatomical patency of the canalicular system, lacrimal sac, and nasolacrimal duct. Confirms that the drainage channel is open — the failure in punctal eversion is purely positional.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: After topical anesthetic drops, a fine cannula is introduced into the punctum and saline is irrigated. Patency is confirmed if the patient feels fluid in the throat. Mild pressure sensation is normal; the test takes 2-3 minutes per eye.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies basal and reflex tear production. Helps separate true overproduction from drainage-related epiphora and identifies coexisting dry eye that may need separate management.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small paper strip is placed inside the lower lid for 5 minutes. Mild irritation is common. Results above 15 mm of wetting indicate normal or excessive production; reduced values point to dry eye.

diagnosticTests.test: Corneal staining with fluorescein or lissamine green
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies exposure keratopathy or corneal epithelial breakdown that may accompany severe ectropion or paralytic lagophthalmos and influence urgency of surgical repair.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Painless drop instillation followed by slit-lamp inspection under blue light. Takes 1-2 minutes after staining.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Documents baseline lid position and asymmetry, supports surgical planning, and provides a record for comparing post-operative outcomes.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standardized photographs taken in clinic at primary, upward, and downward gaze. Painless and takes about 5 minutes.

treatmentOverview: Management of punctal eversion follows a stepwise plan governed by symptom severity, anatomic findings, and the underlying cause. Mild involutional cases without significant lid laxity are first managed medically: preservative-free artificial tears 3-4 times daily, warm compresses, gentle massage along the lid margin toward the punctum, and treatment of any contributing dermatitis with low-potency topical corticosteroids or calcineurin inhibitors. Discontinuation of triggering eye drops (especially preserved glaucoma drops containing benzalkonium chloride) often improves cicatricial cases. When symptoms persist after 2-3 months of optimized medical care, or when the eversion is moderate to severe at presentation, surgery is offered. The procedure is chosen by mechanism. For pure punctal eversion without significant lid laxity, a one-, two-, or three-snip punctoplasty (Kelly procedure) enlarges and repositions the punctum opening; this is performed under local anesthetic as an outpatient procedure with 85-95% epiphora improvement reported. For medial ectropion with everted punctum, a medial spindle procedure (Smith) excises a diamond of tarsoconjunctiva below the punctum and rotates the lid inward. For involutional cases with horizontal lid laxity, the medial spindle is combined with a lateral tarsal strip to shorten and re-anchor the lid. Cicatricial cases require addressing the scarred anterior lamella with Z-plasty, full-thickness skin grafting, or midface lift. Paralytic ectropion benefits from gold weight loading of the upper lid plus lower lid tightening; temporary tarsorrhaphy can protect the cornea while facial nerve recovery is awaited. Across published series, combined approaches yield 80-95% epiphora improvement at 6-12 months, with low complication rates when performed by experienced oculoplastic surgeons.

medicalTreatments.name: Preservative-free artificial tears (carmellose 0.5%, sodium hyaluronate, or carbomer gels)
medicalTreatments.description: Stabilize the tear film, reduce reflex tearing from corneal irritation, and protect the corneal surface. Used 3-6 times daily depending on severity. Preservative-free formulations are essential when long-term use is anticipated.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptomatic improvement in 40-60% of mild cases; bridges to surgery in moderate cases without altering the underlying anatomy.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical corticosteroid or calcineurin inhibitor cream for eyelid dermatitis
medicalTreatments.description: Short courses of hydrocortisone 1% cream or tacrolimus 0.03% ointment treat coexisting atopic or contact dermatitis that contributes to anterior lamellar contracture. Avoid prolonged steroid use on lid skin due to skin atrophy risk.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces lid skin inflammation in 70-80% of patients with active dermatitis; can stabilize and occasionally reverse mild cicatricial eversion.

medicalTreatments.name: Warm compresses and lid massage
medicalTreatments.description: Twice-daily warm compresses (10 minutes) loosen meibomian secretions and reduce lid margin inflammation. Gentle inward-and-upward massage at the inner canthus may aid lacrimal drainage and reduce stasis.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Adjunctive; reduces discharge and lid margin inflammation in 50-70% but rarely corrects the underlying malposition.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical antibiotic ointment for nocturnal use
medicalTreatments.description: Erythromycin ointment or chloramphenicol ointment at bedtime treats coexisting blepharitis, reduces lid margin bacterial load, and provides protective lubrication overnight, especially valuable in paralytic cases.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces secondary infection and conjunctivitis in 60-80% of cases; not curative for the eversion itself.

medicalTreatments.name: Switch to preservative-free chronic eye drops
medicalTreatments.description: Patients on long-term glaucoma drops or dry-eye therapy should be switched to preservative-free or BAK-free alternatives if eyelid skin shows contact dermatitis. Reduces inflammatory contribution to cicatricial eversion.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Improves lid skin appearance and reduces eversion progression in 60-70% of contact-dermatitis-related cases.

medicalTreatments.name: Botulinum toxin for selected cases
medicalTreatments.description: Off-label use to weaken the lower preseptal orbicularis or address protractor-retractor imbalance in some paralytic cases. Limited evidence; reserved for specialist setting.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Modest symptomatic relief in selected paralytic patients; effect lasts 3-4 months.

surgicalOptions.name: Punctoplasty (one-, two-, or three-snip / Kelly procedure)
surgicalOptions.description: Outpatient procedure under local anesthetic. The everted punctum is enlarged with one, two, or three small triangular incisions through the posterior wall of the ampulla, improving tear entry. The three-snip variant (Kelly 1958) removes a triangle of posterior canalicular wall and is preferred for moderate eversion.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 85-95% improvement in epiphora at 6-12 months in published series; recurrence in 5-10% requiring revision.

surgicalOptions.name: Medial spindle procedure (Smith)
surgicalOptions.description: Diamond-shaped excision of tarsoconjunctiva below the punctum, closed with absorbable sutures placed to rotate the lid margin inward. Targets medial ectropion specifically, repositioning the punctum against the globe.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Effective in 80-90% of medial ectropion cases; commonly combined with lateral tarsal strip for involutional eversion.

surgicalOptions.name: Lateral tarsal strip combined with medial spindle
surgicalOptions.description: Gold standard for involutional punctal eversion with horizontal lid laxity. The lateral canthal tendon is shortened and re-anchored to the periosteum of the lateral orbital rim, restoring horizontal tension while the medial spindle corrects punctal position.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 85-95% epiphora improvement at one year; durable correction with low complication rates.

surgicalOptions.name: Full-thickness skin graft or Z-plasty for cicatricial ectropion
surgicalOptions.description: Required when anterior lamellar shortage is the dominant problem. Skin is harvested from upper lid, retroauricular, or supraclavicular sites and grafted into the released lower lid. Z-plasty redirects scar lines for smaller contractures.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 75-90% functional and cosmetic improvement; depends on quality of donor tissue and severity of scarring.

surgicalOptions.name: Gold or platinum weight implantation in the upper lid (paralytic cases)
surgicalOptions.description: Addresses lagophthalmos in facial nerve palsy by adding gravitational closure to the upper lid. Frequently combined with lower lid tightening to correct the paralytic punctal eversion.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Effective lid closure in 90% of paralytic cases; durable for years and reversible if facial nerve function recovers.

surgicalOptions.name: Temporary or permanent tarsorrhaphy
surgicalOptions.description: Surgical adhesion of part of the upper and lower lids to narrow the palpebral aperture and protect the cornea. Used as a temporary measure in severe paralytic ectropion while awaiting nerve recovery or as a permanent solution in selected cases.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Highly effective for corneal protection; cosmetic acceptability varies.

surgicalOptions.name: Conjunctivodacryocystorhinostomy (CDCR) with Jones tube
surgicalOptions.description: Reserved for patients with severe punctal or canalicular failure where standard procedures have failed. A glass tube creates a direct conduit from the conjunctival sac to the nasal cavity, bypassing the punctum entirely.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Successful epiphora control in 75-85%; requires lifelong tube maintenance and carries higher complication rates.

alternativeTreatments.name: Lid taping during sleep
alternativeTreatments.description: Soft hypoallergenic tape applied to support the lower lid in paralytic or post-operative patients overnight. Inexpensive and useful while definitive surgery is planned.

alternativeTreatments.name: Moisture-chamber glasses or goggles
alternativeTreatments.description: Glasses with side shields or sleep goggles reduce evaporation and corneal exposure in paralytic cases; can be obtained without prescription and used long-term.

whySeeSpecialist: An oculoplastic surgeon should be involved when epiphora persists beyond 2-3 months of medical therapy, when there is associated lower lid ectropion or lid retraction, when symptoms follow eyelid surgery or trauma, when paralytic causes are suspected, or when there is corneal exposure or vision threat. General ophthalmologists diagnose and grade the eversion and can manage mild cases medically, but surgical correction is performed by oculoplastic specialists familiar with the anatomy of the medial canthus and the spectrum of repair techniques.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for an oculoplastic surgeon with formal fellowship training in oculofacial plastic and reconstructive surgery (ASOPRS in the United States, BOPSS in the United Kingdom). Ask about their annual volume of punctoplasty, medial spindle, and lateral tarsal strip procedures. Experience with both functional (drainage) and reconstructive cicatricial repair is valuable, since many patients need combined approaches. Inquire whether they offer staged surgery and revision should the first procedure underperform.
preventionStrategies: Treat eyelid skin disease early — atopic, contact, and seborrheic dermatitis should not be left to scar the lower lid
Use preservative-free formulations when long-term topical eye medication is needed to avoid contact dermatitis
Wear UV-protective eyewear outdoors to slow age-related dermal elastosis around the eyes
Choose conservative blepharoplasty techniques — avoid aggressive lower lid skin resection, which is a leading iatrogenic cause
Seek prompt assessment after Bell's palsy or facial nerve injury so that lid taping and lubrication start before exposure damage develops
Stop smoking — tobacco accelerates the dermal changes that promote lid laxity
lifestyleModifications: Avoid rubbing the eyes and the lower lid skin, which accelerates anterior lamellar laxity and scarring
Apply lubricating drops before activities that worsen tearing — outdoor walking in wind, screen-heavy work, and driving
Sleep with the head slightly elevated to reduce overnight tear pooling and inner canthal skin maceration
Use gentle fragrance-free skincare on the eyelid skin; avoid scrubs and harsh cleansers
Apply broad-spectrum sunscreen formulated for the eye area to slow UV damage that contributes to laxity
Address obstructive sleep apnea if floppy eyelid syndrome is suspected — the lid laxity often improves with CPAP therapy

recommended: Adequate hydration with at least 1.5-2 liters of water daily to support tear film quality
Omega-3-rich foods (oily fish, flaxseed, walnuts) which improve meibomian gland secretion and tear film stability
Vitamin A sources (carrots, sweet potatoes, leafy greens) supporting healthy ocular surface epithelium
Vitamin C-rich foods (citrus, peppers) supporting collagen integrity in periocular skin
Balanced protein intake supporting tissue repair after surgery
avoid: Excessive alcohol, which worsens dehydration and tear film instability
High-salt diets that exacerbate periorbital edema in some patients
Crash diets in older adults — rapid facial fat loss can unmask or worsen lid laxity
Smoking, which directly accelerates periocular skin aging and elastin breakdown

exerciseGuidelines: There are no general exercise restrictions. After punctoplasty or lid surgery, avoid heavy lifting, straining, and contact sports for 2 weeks to reduce bleeding and wound dehiscence risk. Swimming should be deferred for 2-3 weeks post-operatively to lower infection risk. Gentle walking and routine activity can be resumed within a day or two. In paralytic cases with lagophthalmos, eye protection during outdoor and dusty activity is essential.
dailyManagement: Instill preservative-free artificial tears 3-4 times daily and a lubricating ointment at bedtime in moderate and paralytic cases
Gently clean the lid margin morning and evening with warm water and soft pads to reduce discharge buildup
Apply a thin barrier emollient (petrolatum or zinc oxide) to the macerated inner canthal skin if dermatitis is present
Use moisture-chamber glasses or wraparound sunglasses outdoors to limit evaporation and reflex tearing
Attend follow-up at the recommended interval (3-6 months) so progression is detected and timed for surgical intervention if needed
prognosis: The functional prognosis of punctal eversion is excellent with appropriate treatment. Mild involutional cases respond to lubrication and dermatitis management in roughly half of patients without surgery. Once surgical correction is required, modern punctoplasty, medial spindle, and combined approaches deliver 80-95% epiphora improvement at 6-12 months across multiple published series and systematic reviews. Long-term durability is good — most patients remain symptomatically improved at 5 years — though age-related lid laxity continues to progress and a minority require revision. Paralytic cases depend largely on whether facial nerve function recovers; up to 70% of Bell's palsy patients regain enough orbicularis tone for the lid position to normalize, while persistent palsy benefits from definitive reconstructive surgery. Cicatricial cases have the most variable outcome, particularly where the underlying skin disease persists. With effective treatment, secondary complications such as exposure keratitis, chronic conjunctivitis, and social embarrassment resolve, and quality-of-life scores improve substantially.
recoveryTimeline: After punctoplasty, mild discomfort and bruising settle within 5-7 days; symptomatic improvement in tearing is often noticed within 2-4 weeks. After medial spindle or combined lateral tarsal strip surgery, swelling peaks at 48-72 hours and resolves over 2-3 weeks; sutures are absorbable in most cases. Final cosmetic and functional results are assessed at 3 months. Cicatricial repair with skin grafting takes longer — the graft takes 7-10 days to vascularize, full integration 6-8 weeks, and final outcome at 6 months. Patients return to most activities within a few days but avoid swimming and contact sports for 2-3 weeks. Eyelid taping at night may be advised for the first 1-2 weeks post-operatively.
complications: Chronic conjunctivitis and recurrent bacterial infection from impaired tear clearance
Eyelid skin maceration and chronic contact dermatitis at the inner canthus from constant tear contact
Exposure keratopathy and corneal ulceration in severe ectropion or paralytic cases, with potential vision loss if untreated
Recurrent eversion after surgical correction in roughly 5-10% of cases, requiring revision
Excessive narrowing or scarring of the punctum (punctal stenosis) after over-aggressive punctoplasty
Persistent social discomfort and depressive symptoms from visible chronic tearing prior to definitive treatment

supportResources.name: American Academy of Ophthalmology — Patient Education
supportResources.description: Authoritative patient information on eyelid disorders, lacrimal drainage problems, and oculoplastic procedures.

supportResources.name: American Society of Ophthalmic Plastic and Reconstructive Surgery (ASOPRS)
supportResources.description: Find fellowship-trained oculoplastic surgeons in the United States and Canada and access patient resources.

supportResources.name: Royal College of Ophthalmologists (UK) — Patient Information
supportResources.description: Patient leaflets on watery eye, ectropion, and lid surgery from the UK professional body.

supportResources.name: National Eye Institute (NEI) — Eyelid and Tear Disorders
supportResources.description: US National Institutes of Health patient education on eye health, drainage problems, and common procedures.

confusedWithConditions.name: Allergic Rhinitis (Hay Fever)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Allergic rhinitis causes bilateral watery eyes accompanied by sneezing, nasal congestion, and itching, peaks seasonally, and responds to antihistamines. In punctal eversion the tearing is typically unilateral or asymmetric, persistent year-round, and the punctum is visibly malpositioned on slit-lamp examination.

confusedWithConditions.name: Bell's Palsy
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Bell's palsy is a cause of punctal eversion rather than a separate diagnosis. It produces sudden unilateral facial weakness with eyebrow droop, inability to close the eye, and dry mouth alongside the watery eye. Punctal eversion can persist after the facial weakness recovers.

confusedWithConditions.name: Allergic Contact Dermatitis of the Eyelids
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Eyelid contact dermatitis presents with itchy, scaly, red eyelid skin and may cause secondary watering, but the punctum is not malpositioned. A patch test identifies the trigger. Untreated chronic dermatitis can progress to cicatricial punctal eversion.

confusedWithConditions.name: Chronic Sinusitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Chronic sinusitis causes facial pressure, postnasal drip, and sometimes mild reflex tearing during exacerbations; the punctum is normally positioned and the tearing is incidental. In punctal eversion the watery eye is the dominant symptom and the punctum is visibly everted.

relatedConditions.name: Bell's Palsy
relatedConditions.relevance: Bell's palsy is one of the main paralytic causes of punctal eversion; up to 30% of patients have persistent lid malposition at one year.

relatedConditions.name: Atopic Dermatitis (Eczema)
relatedConditions.relevance: Chronic eyelid eczema produces skin contracture and is a leading driver of cicatricial punctal eversion.

relatedConditions.name: Allergic Contact Dermatitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Contact dermatitis from cosmetics and preserved eye drops contributes to lid skin contracture and punctal malposition.

relatedConditions.name: Seborrheic Dermatitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Chronic seborrheic blepharitis irritates and thickens the lid margin and contributes to lid laxity in older adults.

relatedConditions.name: Herpes Simplex Infection
relatedConditions.relevance: Herpes simplex blepharitis and conjunctivitis can scar the eyelid skin and lid margin, contributing to cicatricial punctal eversion in chronic cases.

faqs.question: What is punctal eversion?
faqs.answer: Punctal eversion is when the small tear-drainage opening at the inner corner of the lower eyelid rotates outward and no longer touches the eye. Tears cannot enter the drainage system and overflow onto the cheek. The problem is positional, not obstructive.

faqs.question: Why do my eyes water all the time?
faqs.answer: Constant watery eyes in adults are most often caused by a drainage problem, not excess tear production. Common causes include punctal eversion, lower lid ectropion, blocked tear ducts, dry eye with reflex tearing, allergy, and chronic blepharitis.

faqs.question: Is punctal eversion serious?
faqs.answer: Mild punctal eversion is not vision-threatening, but persistent tearing causes lid skin breakdown, recurrent conjunctivitis, and social distress. Severe paralytic cases can expose and damage the cornea. Most cases improve significantly with treatment.

faqs.question: How is punctal eversion diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is made at slit-lamp examination by an ophthalmologist. The punctum is inspected for outward rotation, lid laxity is tested with snap-back and distraction tests, and tear drainage is assessed with fluorescein dye disappearance and saline syringing.

faqs.question: Can punctal eversion get better on its own?
faqs.answer: Mild cases sometimes stabilize with lubricating drops and treatment of eyelid skin disease. Established structural eversion does not usually self-correct. Paralytic cases often improve as facial nerve function recovers.

faqs.question: What surgery treats punctal eversion?
faqs.answer: The most common procedure is punctoplasty, where one to three small snips enlarge and reposition the punctum. Moderate cases need a medial spindle, and lax lower lids benefit from a combined lateral tarsal strip. Cicatricial cases may need skin grafting.

faqs.question: How successful is punctoplasty surgery?
faqs.answer: Published series report 80-95% improvement in epiphora at 6-12 months, with higher success when combined with lid-tightening in patients with horizontal lid laxity. Recurrence requiring revision occurs in roughly 5-10% of patients.

faqs.question: Is punctal eversion surgery painful?
faqs.answer: Punctoplasty is performed under local anesthetic as outpatient surgery. Patients feel pressure and brief stinging when the anesthetic is injected; the procedure itself is painless. Mild discomfort and bruising for 3-5 days are well controlled with paracetamol.

faqs.question: How long is recovery after punctoplasty?
faqs.answer: Most patients return to work within 2-3 days. Bruising and swelling settle within a week. Improvement in tearing is noticed within 2-4 weeks, with final results judged at 3 months. Heavy lifting and swimming are avoided for 2-3 weeks.

faqs.question: Can Bell's palsy cause watery eyes?
faqs.answer: Yes. Bell's palsy weakens the orbicularis oculi muscle, which normally holds the lower lid and punctum against the eye. The punctum rotates outward and tears overflow. Up to 30% of Bell's palsy patients still have a malpositioned lid at one year.

faqs.question: Does eyelid surgery cause punctal eversion?
faqs.answer: Aggressive lower lid blepharoplasty with excess skin removal can scar the eyelid and pull the lid outward, causing punctal eversion. The risk is reported in 1-5% of transcutaneous lower blepharoplasty cases. Experienced surgeons and conservative techniques reduce risk.

faqs.question: Can dry eye cause watery eyes?
faqs.answer: Yes. Dry eye triggers reflex tearing from corneal irritation, which can overwhelm a weak drainage system. Some dry-eye patients paradoxically complain of constant tearing. Treating the underlying dry eye with lubricants usually improves the symptom.

faqs.question: Are there non-surgical options for punctal eversion?
faqs.answer: Non-surgical options include preservative-free artificial tears, warm compresses, topical creams for eyelid dermatitis, switching to preservative-free chronic eye drops, and lid taping at night. These help mild cases but rarely correct established structural eversion.

faqs.question: What happens if punctal eversion is left untreated?
faqs.answer: Untreated punctal eversion leads to chronic tearing, repeated conjunctivitis, skin breakdown at the inner corner, and social embarrassment. Severe paralytic cases can expose and ulcerate the cornea, threatening vision. Most cases progress over months to years.

faqs.question: Who should I see for watery eyes?
faqs.answer: Start with a general ophthalmologist who can examine the lids, test tear drainage, and treat simple cases. If surgery is needed, the ophthalmologist refers to an oculoplastic surgeon, a specialist with fellowship training in eyelid and lacrimal surgery.

faqs.question: Is punctal eversion the same as a blocked tear duct?
faqs.answer: No. A blocked tear duct is an obstruction within the drainage channel itself, diagnosed by failed saline syringing. Punctal eversion is positional — the channel is open, but tears cannot enter the punctum because it faces the wrong way.

faqs.question: Can I prevent punctal eversion?
faqs.answer: Most age-related cases cannot be fully prevented. Treating eyelid dermatitis early, avoiding aggressive lower lid surgery, using preservative-free eye drops, UV protection, and stopping smoking all reduce risk. Prompt assessment after Bell's palsy also helps.

faqs.question: Does punctal eversion affect both eyes?
faqs.answer: Involutional age-related eversion is often bilateral but typically asymmetric, with one eye more affected than the other. Paralytic cases following Bell's palsy or facial nerve injury are usually one-sided. Cicatricial and post-surgical cases depend on the location of the underlying scarring.

faqs.question: Can children get punctal eversion?
faqs.answer: Yes, but it is rare. Congenital cases are seen in Down syndrome, severe ichthyosis, and blepharophimosis syndrome. Acquired pediatric cases follow eyelid trauma or severe atopic dermatitis. Treatment is usually delayed until lid growth allows safe correction.

faqs.question: Will my watery eye affect my vision permanently?
faqs.answer: Mild cases cause blurring only during tearing episodes; vision returns to baseline once tears clear. Severe untreated cases with corneal exposure can cause permanent corneal scarring and reduced vision. Timely treatment prevents this outcome.

faqs.question: How much does punctoplasty cost?
faqs.answer: In most public healthcare systems (UK NHS, Canadian Medicare, Indian government hospitals) punctoplasty for functional epiphora is covered. Private rates are typically USD 800-2000 in the United States for a unilateral procedure; combined surgeries cost more.

simpleName: Watery eye from outward-rotated tear opening
metaDescription: Punctal eversion (ectropion of lacrimal punctum): outward-rotated tear opening causing constant watery eye. Diagnosis, surgery, and prognosis reviewed.
